danger_signals.visual_form: Opposing team uniforms and sprites on field
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat to scoring; defensive positioning required

danger_signals.visual_form: Base occupancy visual state on field
danger_signals.meaning: Risk of runs being scored against player's team

safety_signals.visual_form: Team uniform and color identity during selection screens
safety_signals.meaning: Confirmed control and allegiance; player agency established

safety_signals.visual_form: Ball contact and trajectory away from pitcher

faction_indicators.color_identity: Region-specific uniform color (JP vs US variants suggest distinct palette)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opposing team rendered in contrasting uniform colors
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Team dugout position; home plate association

faction_indicators.color_identity: Contrasting uniform palette to player team

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Active scoreboard display; players on field; inning counter visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Teams alternate between offensive and defensive positioning; batter awaits pitch

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Drastically different UI layouts, sandlot field size, opening ceremony presence, title screen imagery, scoreboard design
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Japanese version uses Rock-Paper-Scissors for first-to-bat determination; US version uses bat grab mechanic. Game length differs (variable vs 7 innings)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Team bracket display (smaller in US version); visual indication of remaining competitors
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Bracket shrinks as teams are eliminated; winner advancement visualization

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Game Over screen with victory/defeat designation; ending cutscene (content varies by region)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game state transition to results screen; player progression tracking via password system (US only)

environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version features larger sand area in field, suggesting different aesthetic/cultural emphasis on field composition vs US minimalist approach
Japanese version displays opening ceremony, establishing tournament formality and cultural significance; US version omits this, emphasizing quick-play gameplay
Team bracket size reduction in US version and scoreboard redesign communicates streamlined competitive structure for Western audience
Rock-Paper-Scissors (Japan) vs bat grab (US) reflects cultural game-decision conventions and accessibility preferences

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Batter positioned at home plate awaiting pitch; runners may occupy bases; dugout positioned as active command zone
Pitcher positioned at mound; fielders distributed across field; dugout positioned as secondary zone
Visible occupancy of base positions; ready state for advancing on hit; vulnerability to out plays
Visual persistence at plate until three strikes; dismissal from game upon strike-out

notes: Little League Baseball: Championship Series demonstrates significant regional visual communication strategies. The Japanese version emphasizes ceremonial/cultural elements (opening ceremony, Rock-Paper-Scissors determination, larger sandlot) while US version prioritizes streamlined gameplay efficiency (password saves, 7-inning regulation, bat grab mechanic, reduced bracket). Danger/Safety signals are sport-inherent rather than narrative—conveyed through game state visualization (scoreboard, field positioning, strike count). Faction identity communicated purely through uniform color differentiation and field positioning. World-state manifestation is highly visual, with UI design itself serving as cultural/regional storytelling. The unused Dugout mascot sprite suggests licensing constraints shaped final visual identity. No explicit political situation communication detected; factions are purely competitive sports teams without ideological dimension.
sources_used: tcrf - Little League Baseball: Championship Series (The Cutting Room Floor)
